topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an aggressive, irritating frequency. Elias traced the edge of his textbook cover, the cheap plastic cool beneath his fingertips. Every glance from the nearby students felt like a physical weight pressing down on his shoulders. The accusation, delivered in hushed, serious tones by the professor, had shattered the morning's calm. He kept replaying the moment—the blank stare, the slow, deliberate way the professor had laid out the evidence. A tightness began to coil low in his stomach, a knot that tightened with every shallow breath he took. He wanted to look away, to dissolve into the beige wallpaper of the study carrels, but his gaze kept snagging on the damning paragraph highlighted in red. His hands started to tremble, a fine, almost imperceptible vibration that he tried to mask by gripping his pen so tightly his knuckles turned white.

[story 2]

"Are you certain about this, Mr. Vance?" The words echoed in the sterile confines of the faculty office, each syllable sharp and accusatory. Liam sat perfectly still in the hard wooden chair, his spine rigid. He focused intently on a speck of dust dancing in a sunbeam slicing through the blinds. Internally, a churning anxiety began to bubble up, hot and fast. He felt a strange numbness spreading through his limbs, making his movements feel sluggish and disconnected. When the committee member finally finished speaking, Liam pushed his chair back an inch, the slight scrape of the wood against the linoleum sounding deafeningly loud. He swallowed hard, his throat suddenly dry, and managed a reply that sounded flat, devoid of any real inflection.

[story 3]

The silence in his dorm room was oppressive, thick enough to choke on. Maya stared at the laptop screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the untouched essay document. She kept running her fingers through her hair, a repetitive, frantic motion that served as a distraction from the churning in her chest. Every time she glanced at the notification on her phone—a text message she hadn't read—a wave of cold dread washed over her. She stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between her desk and the window. Her footsteps sounded hollow on the cheap carpet. She stopped by the window, looking out at the darkened campus grounds, feeling profoundly adrift, as if the solid ground beneath her feet had suddenly liquefied.
